1: Hear this, O priests! Listen, O house of Israel! Give ear, O house of the king! — chapter 5 addresses the entire leadership triad: priests (religious), people (corporate Israel), and king (political). The escalating address draws each level of society into the covenant lawsuit. For judgment is coming against you; you have been a snare at Mizpah, a net spread upon Tabor — Mizpah was both a judicial center and a shrine; Tabor was a mountain with pagan associations. The places where Israel should have found justice and worship have become traps for the people.
2: The rebels are deep in their corruption; though I have been a discipline to all of them — shachatu (deep corruption, slaughter) describes the thoroughness of the apostasy; the word suggests a pit dug as a trap. Despite YHWH’s repeated disciplinary actions in history — drought, plague, enemy invasion, political instability — the leadership has only deepened its rebellion. The history of the northern kingdom from Jeroboam I to the final kings is a history of YHWH’s warnings going unheeded.
3: I know Ephraim, and Israel is not hidden from me — divine omniscience (daʿat again — YHWH “knows” in the same full relational sense Israel refuses to know him). The claim that Israel’s unfaithfulness is hidden — perhaps by the proliferation of cult sites that blended YHWH and Baal worship — is categorically rejected. Ephraim, you have now played the whore; Israel is defiled (tame) — ritual impurity (tumeʾah) that prevents access to YHWH’s presence; the nation has made itself ritually incapable of approaching the very deity it supposedly worships at the shrines.
4: Their deeds do not permit them to return to their God; a spirit of prostitution is within them, and they do not know the LORD — the ruach zenunim (spirit of prostitution, cf. Hosea 4:12) has become so embedded in Israel’s corporate character that repentance is practically impossible — not because YHWH refuses it but because the nation’s habits of mind and practice have foreclosed it. The “knowledge of God” — the relational covenant intimacy — is absent precisely because Israel’s practices have substituted everything else for it. Paul describes the same dynamic in Romans 1: when humans suppress the truth about God, the suppression becomes self-reinforcing.
5: Israel’s arrogance (gaʾon Yisraʾel) testifies against him to his face — gaʾon (pride, arrogance) is the attitude that makes repentance impossible: the refusal to acknowledge dependence on YHWH. The same phrase “pride of Israel” appears in Amos 8:7 as a divine oath (YHWH swears by “the pride of Jacob” = himself, or ironically by Israel’s arrogance). Israel and Judah both stumble in their iniquity — the southern kingdom is also implicated, perhaps in the context of the Syro-Ephraimite war (735 BCE), when Judah allied with Assyria rather than trusting YHWH (Isa 7-8).
6: They will go with their flocks and herds to seek the LORD, but they will not find him; he has withdrawn from them — the bringing of sacrificial animals is the conventional form of seeking YHWH’s favor, but the ritual is futile when it masks the absence of genuine covenant faithfulness. He has withdrawn from them (chalats mibbahem — he has removed himself) — the divine withdrawal as judgment. This is the condition described in Lamentations 3:8 (“when I call and cry for help, he shuts out my prayer”) and contrasts with Deuteronomy 4:29 which promises that genuine seeking in exile will find YHWH — but genuine seeking involves the “acknowledgment of guilt” that Hosea 5:15 specifies.
7: They have been unfaithful to the LORD, because they have borne illegitimate children (banim zarim, “strange/foreign children”) — children of the Baal unions (both literal offspring of cultic sexual activity and metaphorical descendants who are spiritually foreign to YHWH’s covenant). Now the new moon will devour them along with their fields — the new moon festival (which should be a celebration of covenant renewal) becomes an instrument of judgment. The irony: the liturgical calendar Israel has perverted turns against them.
8: Sound the ram’s horn in Gibeah, the trumpet in Ramah; raise the battle cry at Beth-aven — Gibeah, Ramah, and Beth-aven are in Benjamin and at the border between the northern and southern kingdoms. The alarm call suggests an imminent military invasion from the south (Judah, allied with Assyria?) or the Assyrian advance. The archaic battle cry “Behind you, Benjamin!” (ʾachareka Binyamin) may be a quotation from the Song of Deborah (Judges 5:14) or a standard military rally call — its archaism heightens the urgency of the warning.
9: Ephraim will be laid waste on the day of punishment; among the tribes of Israel I have declared what is certain — the “day of punishment” (yom tokechah) is the divinely-appointed judgment; “what is certain” (neʾemanah, faithful/reliable) emphasizes that the oracle is not contingent but fixed. This certainty language is characteristic of Hosea’s judgment oracles in the final chapters of the northern kingdom’s existence: the point of no return has been passed.
10: The princes of Judah have become like those who move boundary stones — moving boundary stones (magge migsug gĕvul) was the most basic violation of property rights in ancient Israel; it was explicitly cursed in Deuteronomy 27:17 and Proverbs 22:28; Hosea 23:10. The princes of Judah are accused of territorial aggression, probably in the context of the Syro-Ephraimite crisis (735 BCE), when Judah’s expansion or political maneuvering violated established boundaries. I will pour out my wrath on them like water — the divine judgment is as unstoppable and overwhelming as a flood.
11: Ephraim is oppressed and crushed in justice, because he was determined to pursue worthless idols — the word translated “worthless idols” or “filth” (tsav) may refer to a specific Baal idol or simply to idolatrous practices in general. The oppression and crushing is both the divine judgment and the historical Assyrian pressure. The phrase “determined to pursue” (yahel halakh ʾachar tsav, “he was stubborn to walk after vanity”) emphasizes willful persistence in the face of repeated warnings.
12: I will be like a moth to Ephraim and like rot to the house of Judah — YHWH as slow internal destruction rather than sudden military defeat. The moth that silently eats the garment and the rot that gradually destroys wood are agents of invisible, cumulative decay. This contrasts with the lion imagery in Hosea 5:14-15: YHWH can act as the hidden agent of dissolution or the sudden agent of catastrophe. The slow decay corresponds to the internal political instability of both kingdoms in the mid-8th century BCE.
13: When Ephraim saw his sickness and Judah saw his wound, Ephraim turned to Assyria and sent to the great king — the historical anchor: Menahem of Israel paid tribute to Tiglath-pileser III of Assyria (745-727 BCE; 2 Kings 15:19-20) in exchange for political support. Ahaz of Judah also appealed to Assyria for help against the Syro-Ephraimite coalition (2 Kings 16:7-9). The great king (melek yarev) may be a title (Assyrian kings used “great king” as a royal title in their inscriptions) or a personal name. But he cannot heal you or cure your wound — the theological verdict: Assyria can temporarily relieve political pressure but cannot address the covenant wound that is the real sickness.
14: For I will be like a lion to Ephraim, like a young lion to the house of Judah; I myself will tear them apart and go away — the divine-warrior-as-predator imagery: YHWH tears and withdraws, leaving his prey to lie wounded. The lion was the most fearsome predator in the ancient Near East and a common symbol of royalty and divine power (cf. Amos 3:8; Revelation 5:5). The withdrawal after tearing — I will carry off and no one shall rescue — describes the completeness of the Assyrian deportation: the northern kingdom’s population was scattered and no power could restore them.
15: I will return to my place until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face; in their distress they will earnestly seek me — YHWH announces withdrawal to “his place” (mĕqomi) with a condition for return: yiʾshemu (acknowledge their guilt, literally “bear their guilt”) and genuine seeking of YHWH’s face. This is the pattern of divine hiddenness that the prophets consistently treat as the prelude to restoration: authentic repentance arising from “distress” (batstsar lahem) rather than ritual seeking (Hosea 5:6). Jesus enacts the same pattern in Matthew 23:39: he withdraws from Jerusalem, conditioning his visible return on Israel saying “Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.”
